what_manipulated: self-construal (independent vs. interdependent orientation) and group size (small vs. large)
what_measured: choice made on behalf of self and multiple others for a shared outcome
experiment: Six studies across different choice contexts (wine, books, movies) in which a decision-maker selects an option that will be shared by a group. The decision-maker's self-construal is measured or varied, and group size is manipulated (small vs. large). Choices are analyzed for the degree to which they reflect self versus others' preferences. Mediation analysis tests whether differential attention to others explains the combined effect of self-construal and group size on choice.

what_manipulated: self-concept clarity (low vs. high uncertainty about identity) and subscription type (identity-relevant vs. identity-irrelevant)
what_measured: choice to keep or quit an ongoing subscription; willingness to adopt a new subscription
experiment: Units with varying self-concept clarity are presented with decisions to continue or discontinue subscriptions, and to adopt new subscriptions. The study distinguishes identity-relevant from identity-neutral subscriptions. Analysis tests whether low self-concept clarity predicts subscription retention for identity-relevant services and whether the effect differs from one-time purchases. Additional analyses examine whether the mechanism involves threat to self-concept stability.

what_manipulated: product anthropomorphism (humanized vs. non-humanized product options)
what_measured: comparative judgment strategy and resulting preference (absolute vs. dimension-by-dimension comparison)
experiment: Six experiments in which units compare two options presented as either anthropomorphized or non-anthropomorphized. Choices and information-processing behavior are measured via self-report, MouseLab software tracking, and eye-tracking. Analysis examines whether anthropomorphism shifts judgment strategy from dimension-by-dimension to holistic absolute judgment. Mediation tests perception of products as integrated entities. Moderation by motivation for accuracy or ease is examined.

what_manipulated: question topic: objective fact with partisan salience (inauguration crowd size comparison)
what_measured: likelihood of deliberately providing false response vs. stating genuine belief
experiment: Survey of units with varying political affiliation and engagement. A factual question with an objectively verifiable answer (crowd size comparison) that has high partisan salience is asked. The premise is that if responses are false despite the answer being clear and obvious, this indicates intentional expressive responding rather than genuine misperception. False responses are correlated with partisan identity and political interest level to assess the role of partisan motivation.

what_manipulated: probability of being allowed to trade (randomized assignment; low vs. high probability)
what_measured: choice to keep or trade item; willingness-to-accept valuation for item
experiment: Two experiments: (1) Exchange experiment in which units are endowed with an item and randomly assigned a probability (low vs. high) of being permitted to trade it away; retention choice is measured. (2) Valuation experiment in which units are randomly assigned a high or low probability of obtaining an item and then give their willingness-to-accept selling price. Analysis tests whether higher-probability treatments shift reference points, resulting in higher valuations or lower retention.

what_manipulated: random assignment to position in income distribution (last place vs. other positions)
what_measured: gamble choice; allocation decisions in distributional games; survey attitudes toward income redistribution policy
experiment: Laboratory experiments with three components: (1) Gamble task in which units are randomly placed in different positions in a distribution and choose between gambles; (2) Modified dictator game in which units in second-to-last position allocate money to the person above or below them; (3) Analysis of survey data correlating position in income distribution with stated preferences for redistribution policy. Tests whether units more often choose upward-moving gambles and upward-directed giving when in last place.

what_measured: high school completion; college entry; college completion
experiment: Longitudinal study with units followed from primary school through college. Causal effect of grade retention is estimated using propensity score matching and sibling fixed-effects models to control for background confounds. Mediators (middle school achievement, high school expectations) are examined. Analysis traces when and through which mechanisms units recover from or persist with the scarring effect of early retention.

what_measured: preference for helping colleagues vs. career advancement vs. company innovation
experiment: Units (N=555) are randomly assigned to one of three time-horizon conditions (control: unspecified; experimental: expanded or limited horizons). Units then choose from three work-activity options and indicate their preference. Age is the predictor of interest. Analysis tests whether age predicts helping-colleague preference only under unspecified time horizons, and whether time-horizon framing eliminates age differences.

what_manipulated: intensive behavioral treatment (40 hours/week EIBT vs. standard special education classes)
what_measured: IQ; language development; nonverbal skill; adaptive behavior; educational placement level
experiment: A 3-year prospective outcome study comparing two groups: (1) 21 units receiving intensive behavioral treatment (35-40 hours/week), and (2) 21 matched units in special education classes (assignment based on parental preference, not randomization). Independent examiners assess units at baseline and years 1-3 using standardized instruments (IQ, language, nonverbal skills, adaptive behavior). Repeated-measures analyses of covariance test whether treatment group gains exceed control group gains. Final educational placement is recorded.

what_manipulated: presence or absence of a weak electrical prepulse preceding an intense electrical shock (prepulse at 40-60 ms before shock; at perceptual or 25% above threshold)
what_measured: rated pain intensity of electrical shocks
experiment: Units first have their pain threshold determined via electrical stimulation. Units then rate the painfulness of 25 intense electrical shocks (1.5x pain threshold, ~160V) presented either alone or preceded by weak electrical prepulses. Prepulse effectiveness is compared across units stratified by pain sensitivity. Analysis tests whether prepulses reduce rated pain and whether the effect is stronger in units with lower pain thresholds.

what_manipulated: action structure (joint action vs. individual action) in a goal-pursuit task
what_measured: preference for joint vs. individual action; effort expended on joint vs. individual task
experiment: Five experiments in which units perform goal-achievement tasks that can be completed either jointly with another unit or individually. The joint action option is costlier (requires more effort) than individual action but achieves the same outcome. A utility-based approach measures units' preference for performing actions together despite the higher instrumental cost. Experiments use different task types and measure both choice and actual effort allocation.

what_manipulated: none (observational study of personality traits via self- and partner-rating)
experiment: Pairs of units (74 married, 136 dating couples) provide self-ratings and cross-ratings of their own and partner's personality traits (Big Five and affectivity). Each unit's relationship satisfaction is then correlated with four sets of personality ratings: own self-rated traits, partner's rating of own traits, partner's self-rated traits, and own rating of partner's traits. Regression models test which personality dimensions best predict satisfaction and whether self- and partner-ratings yield similar predictive patterns.

what_manipulated: none (measurement and exploratory factor analysis of spirituality constructs)
what_measured: dimensions of spirituality expressions; relationship to Big Five personality model
experiment: Study 1 (N=534): factor analysis on 11 existing spirituality measures to identify latent dimensions. Study 2 (N=938): replication of factor structure and development of a new 98-item instrument (ESI) to operationalize the spirituality model. Correlations between ESI subscales and the NEO Personality Inventory-Revised are computed to test whether spirituality dimensions are unique from Big Five traits. Reliability and validity of the ESI are assessed.

what_manipulated: outcome affective content (affect-rich vs. affect-poor prizes) and probability (low vs. high)
what_measured: preference between a high-probability affect-poor outcome and a low-probability affect-rich outcome
experiment: Units make a series of risky choices between lotteries that vary in prize type (affect-rich outcomes like kisses or shocks vs. affect-poor monetary outcomes) and probability. Analysis tests the prediction that probability-sensitivity functions (weighting functions) will be more S-shaped for affect-rich than affect-poor outcomes. A probability-outcome interaction is examined: affect-poor prizes are preferred under certainty but lose preference at low probabilities.

what_manipulated: contingency between conditioned stimuli (novel objects) and unconditioned stimuli (valenced items: positive vs. negative)
what_measured: attitude toward novel conditioned stimuli (explicit and implicit measures)
experiment: Two experiments in which units view hundreds of randomly presented words and images, with critical pairings of novel objects (conditioned stimuli) with positive or negative items (unconditioned stimuli). In a surprise evaluation task, attitudes toward the novel objects are measured via both explicit rating and implicit assessment. A covariation estimation task tests whether units explicitly remember the pairings. Analysis examines whether paired valence shifts attitudes toward the neutral novel objects despite lack of explicit memory for the pairings.
